intro:
Macedonia was a Roman province in the northern Aegean region — roughly the territory of modern northern Greece and the southern Balkans — lying north of Achaia and west of Thrace. It had been the home of Alexander the Great, whose conquests spread Greek culture across the eastern world. By the New Testament period it was organized as a Roman senatorial province with Thessalonica as its capital. Paul received the Macedonian call in a night vision at Troas (Acts 16:9–10), crossing from Asia into Europe to bring the gospel there for the first time.

Paul's ministry in Macedonia produced three significant churches: Philippi (Acts 16:11–40), Thessalonica (Acts 17:1–9), and Berea (Acts 17:10–14). He revisited the province multiple times (Acts 20:1–3; 2 Corinthians 2:13; 7:5) and held the Macedonian churches up as models of generosity, noting that despite their deep poverty they gave liberally to the collection for the Jerusalem poor (2 Corinthians 8:1–5; Romans 15:26). The two letters to the Thessalonians and the letter to the Philippians address Macedonian congregations directly.

Macedonia holds a singular place in redemptive history as the threshold of the gospel's entry into Europe. The vision of a man pleading, "Come over to Macedonia and help us" (Acts 16:9), turned Paul westward across the Aegean, and at Philippi a businesswoman, a slave girl, and a jailer were among the firstfruits of Christ on the continent. Out of deep poverty the Macedonian churches gave with overflowing joy (2 Corinthians 8:2), and to the Philippians Paul wrote his most joyful letter, bidding them "Rejoice in the Lord always" (Philippians 4:4). So a province once famed for Alexander's conquests became renowned for a far greater conquest — the advance of the gospel of Christ among the nations of the West.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Philippi
First church in Europe; Lydia and the jailer believed (Acts 16:12)
Thessalonica
Provincial capital and an early church (Acts 17:1)
Berea
Whose people searched the Scriptures daily (Acts 17:11)
Neapolis
Port where Paul first landed in Europe (Acts 16:11)
Amphipolis
City Paul passed through toward Thessalonica (Acts 17:1)
Apollonia
A stop on the Macedonian road (Acts 17:1)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Paul
Called by a vision to bring the gospel to Macedonia (Acts 16:9)
Silas
Paul's companion, imprisoned with him at Philippi (Acts 16:25)
Timothy
Co-worker who labored in the Macedonian churches (Acts 17:14)
Luke
The 'we' narrator who joined the crossing to Macedonia (Acts 16:10)
Lydia
Dealer in purple, first European convert (Acts 16:14)
Jason
Thessalonian host dragged before the rulers (Acts 17:6)
Aristarchus
Macedonian who traveled on with Paul (Acts 19:29)
Sopater
Berean who accompanied Paul from Macedonia (Acts 20:4)
Greece
Macedonia was Alexander's homeland and the seat of his empire (Dan 8:21)
Rome
Governed Macedonia as a senatorial province (Acts 16:12)
